title: Apple takes Epic fight over app store fees to the Supreme Court
gist: Apple has petitioned the US Supreme Court to reverse a 9th Circuit contempt finding over its 27% commission on external app-payment link-outs, with the outcome likely to set the global benchmark commission rate regulators (including the UK CMA) are watching.

insights.claim: The US Supreme Court has agreed to hear Apple's challenge to a 9th Circuit contempt finding over App Store commission fees, likely during its term beginning October 2026.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: A definitive ruling on App Store economics is now on a clock; watch October term for a catalyst on Apple services-revenue risk.
insights.locator: On Tuesday, the Supreme Court confirmed

insights.claim: Apple argues the 9th Circuit's 'spirit'-based contempt standard conflicts with other circuits that require violation of explicit language in a judicial order, framing the case as a circuit split on civil contempt.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The circuit-split framing is Apple's strongest path to reversal; if SCOTUS narrows the contempt standard, the commission fight resets in Apple's favor.
insights.quote: Apple clearly could not be held in civil contempt in other circuits for charging a commission that is nowhere mentioned in the underlying order

insights.claim: The 9th Circuit ruled Apple's 27% link-out commission had a 'prohibitive effect' on developers, who saw little benefit in updating apps to save only a small amount on fees.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Courts are scrutinizing effective fee levels, not just formal compliance; a purely nominal fee cut may not satisfy regulators.
insights.locator: At that time, the 9th Circuit concluded

insights.claim: Apple fears that whatever commission rate a US district court sets on remand could become practically impossible to reverse in other jurisdictions monitoring the case, effectively globalizing the US outcome.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The US ruling is a global precedent for Apple services margin; model worst-case commission compression across all markets, not just the US.
insights.quote: that rate may be practically difficult—if not impossible—to change in the other jurisdictions that are closely monitoring these proceedings

insights.claim: The UK's Competition and Markets Authority is preparing to challenge how Apple and Google block link-outs to alternative payment methods, arguing lower fees would benefit competition.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Regulatory pressure on app-store fees is multi-jurisdiction and coordinated; Apple and Google services revenue faces simultaneous US and UK threats.
insights.quote: consumers and app owners are being let down by Apple and Google restrictions on spending money outside their app stores
insights.locator: As the SCOTUS review was announced

insights.claim: Epic expects to force Apple to produce receipts and confidential internal business data justifying why its commission rates are so high, a disclosure Apple hopes to avoid via reversal.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Forced cost disclosure could expose the true margin structure of App Store fees, a downside risk to Apple's negotiating position and services narrative.
insights.quote: confidential business data regarding the company's decision-making concerning the App Store
insights.locator: If the Supreme Court sides with Apple

insights.claim: Apple justifies its commission as compensation for IP-protected tools, arguing even a single in-app Fortnite skin purchase relies on the iPhone screen, touch controls, Apple silicon, dev tools, and App Store platform.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: Apple's whole-stack value argument is the intellectual backbone of its fee defense; its persuasiveness in court determines the floor on commission rates.
insights.quote: ensure that Apple can continue to receive compensation for use of its IP-protected tools, technologies, and services

insights.claim: Both Epic and the Coalition for App Fairness demand that any Apple or Google app-store charges be justified by transparent data on underlying costs, aligning with the CMA's fairness stance.
insights.speaker: Ashley Belanger
insights.so_what: The emerging cross-party demand for cost-based fee justification signals a structural shift toward regulated, transparent app-store economics.
insights.quote: charges should be justified by 'transparent data' from Apple and Google explaining any underlying cost to the tech companies
insights.locator: Meanwhile, the UK is also hoping
